page.content: In the study, the patient number (n), progesterone (mean ± SD), and P value were recorded across the study and control groups. In the study group for Glycodelin, the negative cohort comprised 8 patients with a mean progesterone level of 13.25 ± 10.57 and a P value of 0.346. The positive cohort with a 1+ classification included 10 patients, yielding a mean progesterone of 16.9 ± 6.97. For the positive 2+ classification, there were 12 patients with a mean progesterone of 20.1 ± 5.16. In the control group for Glycodelin, the negative cohort included 3 patients with a mean progesterone of 7.85 ± 7.10 and a P value of 0.542.

In the study group for Glycodelin, the negative cohort comprised 8 patients with a mean progesterone level of 13.25 ± 10.57
and a P value of 0.346. The positive cohort with a 1+ classification included 10 patients, yielding a mean progesterone of 16.9 ±
6.97. For the positive 2+ classification, there were 12 patients with a mean progesterone of 20.1 ± 5.16. In the control group for
Glycodelin, the negative cohort included 3 patients with a mean progesterone of 7.85 ± 7.10 and a P value of 0.542.

page.content: The positive 1+ classification contained 5 patients, resulting in a mean progesterone level of 12.98 ± 1.74. Lastly, the positive 2+ classification had 12 patients with a mean progesterone of 12.59 ± 5.81. The P values for the negative patients in both the study and control groups were 0.346 and 0.542, respectively, indicating no significant difference in progesterone levels between the groups. Overall, the data indicate varying progesterone levels across positive classifications in the study group compared to the control group, with a notable increase in the positive 2+ classification in the study group, reflecting a mean progesterone of 20.1 ± 5.16, which contrasts with the values seen in the control cohorts.
